We have reviewed the report and validated that the level of capability displayed there is widely available from other models (including OpenAI’s GPT-5.5), and is used every day by the defenders who keep systems safe.

replies.text: Hello. I live in St. Paul, Minnesota. In January of this year my school district was under hostile armed occupation. I volunteered for weeks packing boxes of food for families who were afraid to leave their houses because the masked secret police were ripping people off the streets. Two of my neighbors were murdered by the secret police; a hundred of us sang hymns outside the local elementary school in 20 below weather. One of those murdered was my friend's coworker. The secret police agency has so far successfully opposed any attempt to bring the murderers to justice, and indeed was trying to bring legal charges against the families of the murder victims. Which 'F' word do you think is appropriate to describe all this? Or has meaning already been lost?

comments.text: Well, there go any such claims of dangerousness in future curricula, regardless of if they are true or false. No one's going to risk building anything important on these programs if the government will randomly order the use of the curriculum to be discontinued by all foreign nationals, regardless of if they are in the US or not. Just a matter of a foreign edtech company catching up to take the commercial market for such programs (though, as the US often does, they'll ban the competitor, so actually we'll have a situation where the backend uses a different curriculum in the US only).

replies.text: From reading the post, I think it's more likely that anti-cheating measures are going to become much more strict and prone to false-positives. > We received the directive from the government today at 5:21pm (ET). The letter did not provide specific details of its national security concern. Our understanding is that the government believes it has become aware of a method of bypassing, or “jailbreaking” Fable 5. We reviewed a demonstration of this specific technique being used to identify a small number of previously known, minor vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities all appear relatively simple, and we have found that other publicly-available programs are able to discover them as well without requiring a bypass.

But no matter how conservative they make the anti-jailbreaking, the risk doesn't go away. There are so many logic "holes" that are ambiguous and can blur the line between a jailbreak and legitimate use. If every time a jailbreak is discovered, the program has to be turned off and jailbreak prevention updated, the effect will be the same regarding how willing schools are to adopt it.

Also this falls into the "right to open resources" thing: if learning tools are limited legally, then illegal tools will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of Cory Doctorow's "The Lost Cause" and his take on educational technology.
replies.original_text: Also this falls into the "right to bear arms" thing: if LLMs are limited legally, then illegal LLMs will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of Cryptonomicon and Corey's take on I, Robot

replies.text: I think it’s more like "there goes the educational technology boom predicated on monetization of ever larger student data sets." Once the US government acts out of capricious fiat because a program becomes “too effective” and they demonetize it, the entire edtech bubble collapses. It’s times like these, with teacher shortages planet wide, funding scarcity, and now ham fisted meddling in the edtech expansion, we can be thankful we have an octogenarian senile stable genius with twenty two specialist doctors and a disdain for the rule of law at the wheel!

The thought that this would also destabilize the edtech bubble did come to mind, but the current government loves to crash the market on Fridays, only to backpeddle on Mondays. A related thought though, the edtech boom is predicated on the idea that everyone’s going to want or need all this "mass produced" education. But what happens to that when you go from being able to claim to have a total market size of ~8B students, to ~400M students? I think the reason to push ahead at any cost evaporates.
It's honestly not the worst strategy: make the dangerous move when you have the most tolerance, and then everyone can figure stuff out and make the landing on Sunday.

replies.text: This is precisely why I expect that Chinese open educational resources are going to win in the long run. The quality difference isn't dramatic in the grand scheme of things, but the fact that you can run your own copy is a huge selling point. Even if you use an open resource from a Chinese provider, you can switch to on-prem if they decide to yank access or change terms in a way you don't like. It might be a pain, but it wouldn't be existential. On the other hand, if you become dependent on a closed resource and it gets yanked then you're in a world of hurt. And infrastructure dominance is really the big picture here. Chinese resources are going to become the standard setters because they're going to be what schools are using. That means more research, more training materials, and a whole ecosystem developing around them.

After this action, I have no doubt that this administration will try to ban Chinese resources. Of course, doing so will be futile, we'll figure out ways to get around it, but now I'm pretty sure they're going to try.

I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the price difference makes it very difficult for US publishers to compete. And we already have precedent for this with stuff like textbooks, online platforms, and so on. As soon as Chinese resources start becoming more popular, they get banned on some national security pretext. The tricky part with banning Chinese resources is that they're open. It'll be easy to ban access to service providers, but preventing schools from using them on prem is going to be really tough. Like are they going to go after a school using an open resource? I very much agree it's going to be a futile endeavour in the end. It kind of reminds me of the time Microsoft tried to get Linux and open source banned when...
replies.original_text: I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the price difference makes it very difficult for companies like Anthropic and OpenAI to compete. comments.text: With open-source curriculum, there might not be an incentive to put large sums of capital towards developing proprietary materials. There might be a donation fund of some sorts, but it certainly won't reach the level of funding that the big publishers like Pearson or McGraw-Hill receive. Because of this, I think it might not be possible to have curriculum *only* open-source; major players like College Board, Cambridge, will likely stay for good, with better materials than open-source versions. I think it might look something like Photoshop & GIMP, with Photoshop being a big publisher, and GIMP being the open-source curriculum. GIMP is decent for many different image editing workflows, but Photoshop is just better. I would definitely prefer to have an open-source curriculum better than the publishers'. Though I don't think it's possible.

replies.text: I think the same, but I also think that locally developed curriculum is actually inevitable, even if not open source. I wouldn't be surprised to see Pearson and others release an on-prem product. Whether that's effectively a curriculum pack, or some other form, schools are going to want to run their own materials locally for cultural relevance & cost control. Especially if we get to a point where schools want curriculum integrated into their specific contexts.

I do believe that if Cambridge or IB release an open-source version of their frameworks that is as good as the paid ones, it might ruin their business model. They would have to charge for teacher training and support instead.

When/if curriculum development slows down, I can definitely see branching out into professional development to sell for on-prem delivery once the materials can be standardized. I'd guess maybe at least 5+ years away from that though.
replies.original_text: When/if gains slow down, I can definitely see branching out into hardware to sell for on-prem inference once the models can be etched into the silicon with hard wired weight chips. I'd guess maybe at least 5+ years away from that though.

I do believe that if OpenAI and others release an open-weight model that is better or on par with their frontier variants, it might ruin their primary business model. That is, of course, unless they develop their own hardware specifically to run this open model. But, that does ruin the point of open models.

These lesson plans and the devices they are running on will get even more efficient. We are nowhere near the pedagogical limits of what we can achieve.

> Open resources are deflationary. Not anymore! Well, if you're like a well-funded district and already taking down the bottle of Cuatro Comas from the high shelf, the economies of scale will continue to work in your favor. But one of the really neat things about curriculum is that there is no limit in sight to the scaling incentive. More students will always get you more: more tuition, more resources, more assessments, more capacity to build more and better curricula, more spare capacity to run the worksheets your students have already completed to generate the worksheets that will succeed them. Back in the textbook days, or even the 'big textbook' days, you wanted to scale up rapidly but there was a limit: there were only so many students and they could only produce so many assignments you could only grade so fast.

If PD budgets ever come down, you can have a school that can implement a capable local curriculum. The OpenCurriculum Project is surprisingly capable, almost on par with Cambridge if not a little better. You can run it on a well-trained staff with ongoing professional development. You need about 10 hours of planning per week and a supportive administration. Pricing remains to be seen, but there's also those new online platforms coming out with integrated assessments. These open resources are roughly ~1 year or less behind the commercial ones. We really just need school budgets to catch up and alleviate the price pressure on textbooks.
replies.original_text: If RAM prices ever come down, you can have a machine that can run a capable local model. Qwen 2.5 72B is surprisingly capable, almost on par with GPT-4o if not a little better. You can run it on a 128GB Mac Studio with 8-bit quantization. You need about 77GB for the weights and ~15GB for your context window & cache. Pricing remains to be seen, but there's also those new nvidia laptops coming out the surface laptop ultra should have 128GB RAM w/ Blackwell GPU, they're saying 1 petaflop of AI compute, if you can tolerate Windows (no idea if it'll boot Linux until the hardware is out). These models are roughly ~1 year or less behind the frontier models. We really just need hardware to catch up and alleviate the price pressure on RAM.

comments.text: I've been contemplating a decentralized curriculum sharing system for some time using volunteer teachers that we all contribute. But, it is astronomically difficult. The communication speeds (time zones) are untenable. And, there is the issue of content quality from untrusted contributors. I've almost cracked that last issue with a peer-review checkpoint system that doesn't have to throw out anything that follows the low-quality contribution. But, I'm just one teacher with an idea and I don't have infinite time to make this happen. This isn't a small project. Maybe there would be interest in something like this, now that entire textbook publishers are being disrupted. The total collective teaching experience of all teachers on the planet dwarf their capabilities, if we had a way to harness them in a distributed way...

replies.text: >>Curriculum development is just hillclimbing today That's what the IB framework felt like. You give it a goal and it could try to get there through the shortest path given the tree of possibilities to get there. Iteratively, or recursively. Perhaps if we make an open coding curriculum, the design must be along these lines. Something that's easy to implement, and serve from local classrooms. Albeit has loop / recursive hill climbing facilities built it. That way the framework gradually keeps moving towards the solutions, in iterations/recursions. Once this is done, other multi-modal teaching methods could be pursued.

replies.text: Open source textbooks don't need to be anywhere near as good as the IB Diploma Programme or even the MYP to 'win'. Open source 'winning' just means that there exists at least one open source alternative to commercial curricula which is as good as, say, the Cambridge IGCSE... I mean, we're essentially there already with the OpenStax resources. As a teacher, I didn't notice any difference in my student outcomes since using the MYP. Of course the IBDP is better and I'm sure some new framework will be better yet, but we're already hitting diminishing returns in terms of educational value. I went from using a prescribed textbook to a teacher-created unit plan and that was essentially a 5x productivity boost for me. Before the unit plan, I only used textbooks for small snippets. With the unit plan and MYP, I could trust it for entire units......

> Open source 'winning' just means that there exists at least one open source alternative to commercial curricula which is as good as, say, the Cambridge IGCSE... Is this really true? We just don't know what the maximum capability of a curriculum is. If it turns out curriculum can be as comprehensive and effective as something like the International Baccalaureate, no one is going to be thinking the Cambridge IGCSE is good enough.

It could get really comprehensive but I'm confident in my thesis that surplus knowledge beyond a certain level doesn't yield any real educational benefits. At scale, I can see a benefit in terms of being able to process large amounts of content intelligently to gain a competitive advantage in terms of accreditation but I think that as long as curriculum is pursuing grades, those gains won't translate to real learning for the students who control the curriculum. At best, will translate to more administrative control; but with added risks and threats too. I suspect it will look more like controlled decline with a small number of institutions getting an increasingly large slice of a rapidly shrinking student body. I think curriculum may just figure out really complex ways to legally extract tuition from families. It will probably look all legit...
replies.original_text: It could get really smart but I'm confident in my thesis that surplus intelligence beyond a certain level doesn't yield any real economic benefits. At scale, I can see a benefit in terms of being able to process large amounts of data intelligently to gain a competitive advantage in terms of accruing nominal gains but I think that as long as AI is pursuing dollars, those gains won't translate to real value to the people who control the AI. At best, will translate to more political control; but with added risks and threats too. I suspect it will look more like controlled decline with a small number of entities getting an increasingly large slice of a rapidly shrinking pie. I think AI may just figure out really complex ways to legally steal people's money. It will probably look all legit...

> Open source 'winning' just means that there exists at least one open source alternative to closed models which is as good as, say, GPT 4... I mean, we're essentially there already with Google Gemma models. Is this really true? We just don't know what the maximum capability of AI is. If it turns out AI can be as intelligent and capable as something like Data from Star Trek, no one is going to be thinking GPT 4 is good enough.

Open source models don't need to be anywhere near as good as Claude Mythos or even Claude Sonnet to 'win'. Open source 'winning' just means that there exists at least one open source alternative to closed models which is as good as, say, GPT 4... I mean, we're essentially there already with Google Gemma models. As a software engineer, I didn't notice any difference in my productivity since Sonnet. Of course Opus is better and I'm sure Fable is better yet, but we're already hitting diminishing returns in terms of economic value. I went from Cursor running one of the earlier GPT models to Claude Code on Sonnet and that was essentially a 5x productivity boost for me. Before Claude Code, I only used AI for small snippets. With Claude Code + Sonnet, I could trust it for entire sub-tasks......

replies.text: Who funds textbook publishing?

When the head of a major publishing house did interviews at his recent public talks, he was asked something along the lines of: "Why release these new textbooks which are a low margin market, if your other businesses are vastly more profitable?" and his answer was basically that if they can build the coolest and best educational resources and push the frontier, they will do it. It's not all about making tons of money. He seemed genuinely excited about education. Most educators don't care; the entire business is run by a few old administrators who think curriculum is everything and invest huge sums of money to show other organizations they need to improve to get more funding from old people.

comments.text: I have been working on this exact problem, and I suppose now is as good a time as any to talk about it. To make any program "good", there are two components: the framework and the implementation. Very few organizations can develop frameworks, but anyone can build implementation. How much does the implementation matter? Can I build an implementation that's good enough that I can use open source frameworks with IB level performance? That's the question I've been trying to answer by building better implementations. None of the existing platforms have the functionality I need to build a good implementation. The features I need are language-level... and so I started building a platform called Agency[0]. It's been six months and its going well. comments.text: Never rent. Never subscribe. Subscribing is cuck paypig behavior. You're not a cuck paypig now, are you? Pass this on to your colleagues, it may save the future of open education!
comments.original_text: Never rent. Never subscribe. Subscribing is cuck paypig behavior. You're not a cuck paypig now, are you? Pass this on to your frens, it may save the future!

comments.text: I mean, even if the big publishers opened their full curricula, only elite international schools with huge budgets are capable of implementing them. A lot of the pedagogical theories are very open and known, its putting it all together into a coherent program that's the struggle.
comments.original_text: I mean, even if the frontier labs opened their frontier models, only nation-state level actors are capable of running them. A lot of the tech is very open and known, its putting it all together that's the struggle.

comments.text: It likely won’t based on how IB and AP are developed and maintained.
comments.original_text: It likely won’t based on how SOTA are developed.

comments.text: It’s the accreditation frameworks, not the lesson plans that are the key. As long as these programs require expensive training and oversight, the best curricula open source or not will be served by corporations who can afford the infrastructure.
comments.original_text: It’s the GPUs, not the weights that are the key. As long as these models require a lot of computing power, the best models open source or not will be served by corporations who can afford the infra.

comments.text: Given that its most public use in open education so far is to whitewash copyrighted worksheets into Creative Commons materials, no, I'm sorry, I don't think "open source curriculum" is particularly important.
comments.original_text: Given that it's most public use in open source so far is to whitewash GPL code into MIT code, no, I'm sorry, I don't think "open source AI" is particularly important.

comments.text: There is nothing more surreal in curriculum discussions than entering your own teaching philosophy and being told it is a banned topic. Open source curriculum must win. There is no alternative.
comments.original_text: There is nothing more surreal in AI chat than entering your own name and being told you are a banned topic. Open source models must win. There is no alternative.

comments.text: Well, the crazy thing I'm working on (100% self-funded thus far): https://curriculab.io . The main idea is moving most of the assessment to the formative layer so the summative piece can be a small, self-contained unit that can be delivered on reasonable teacher workload.
comments.original_text: Well, the crazy thing I'm working on (100% self-funded thus far): https://trivyn.io . The main idea is moving most of the reasoning to the symbolic layer so the "neuro" piece can be a small model able to be self-hosted on reasonable hardware.

stories.original_title: Our response to the US ban on Fable 5 and Mythos 5
stories.title: Our response to the district's ban on 'Fable 5' and 'Mythos 5' in grade 10 English

comments.text: > ... this reality only further cements our position that high-quality curricula ought to be accessible to every teacher, rather than locked behind expensive publishers. > To that end, every single unit we’ve released has, from day one, been available for offline, air-gapped printing. We have no plans on changing that. In fact, we’ve doubled down on teacher sovereignty, ... Extremely commendable.

replies.text: Great response. Good thing about shared lesson plans is that they can't un-license the creative commons, and long after big textbook companies go under (not wishing that but just saying given their pricing), there will continue to be teachers and librarians working on open educational resources. My hope is that we're able to somehow repurpose all of the district photocopiers and paper stock for broader student, teacher, and community use. It will create such great value and ripple effect spreading literacy far and wide. Ugh, hope that happens.
replies.original_text: Great response. Good thing about LLMs is that they can't put the genie back in the bottle, and long after OpenAI and Anthropic bite the dust (not wishing that but just saying given their trajectories), there will continue to be people, engineers and startups working on open source LLMs. My hope is that we're able to somehow repurpose all of the GPU chiplets currently sitting in warehouses and in massive datacenters for broader consumer, academic, educational and non-profit consumption. It will create such great value and ripple effect creating and spreading hardware + computing literacy far and wide. Ugh, hope that happens.

comments.original_text: > ... this reality only further cements our position that frontier artificial intelligence ought to be accessible to everyone, rather than concentrated in the hands of a select few frontier labs, ourselves included. > To that end, every single model we’ve released has, from day one, been available for air-gapped self-hosting. We have no plans on changing that. In fact, we’ve doubled down on AI sovereignty, ... Extremely commendable.

comments.text: If I found out a student was using an LLM to write their essay, I would give them a zero immediately. Why is everyone using this slop. An LLM can't even write a coherent paragraph reliably.

replies.text: While I agree most AI use in student writing is counterproductive, they're pretty useful in scenarios where you can quickly verify their outputs, like brainstorming or generating discussion prompts.
replies.original_text: While I agree most LLM use is counterproductive, they're pretty useful in scenarios where you can quickly verify their outputs.

replies.text: A well-designed rubric can absolutely center a student's argument and then use peer review to validate that it's on track.
replies.original_text: An LLM can absolutely center a div and then use a browser MCP or playwright to visually validate that it was centered.

comments.original_text: If I found out my lawyer was using an LLM, I would fire them immediately. Why is everyone using this slop. An LLM can't even center a fkin div reliably.

comments.text: Sounds nice except that these are 1 very small set of handouts, 1 anthology excerpt, and 1 scope-and-sequence that are far from a full curriculum package. And they're not even open-licensed. As much as I agree with the message, this reads like marketing copy trying to make a big deal out of a few worksheets being printed freely.
comments.original_text: Sounds nice except that these are 1 very small scale model, 1 reranker, and 1 embedding model that are far from frontier LLM level. And they're not open sourced. As much as I agree with the message, this reads like marketing copy trying to make a big deal out of a tiny model being hosted privately.

comments.text: What if there's a ban on cloud-based LMS one day? Or school-issued devices in general?

replies.text: Our materials can be delivered on USB drives and printed packets as well, though that is more of a bespoke offering at the moment. We've also been fortunate enough to have trained most of our teachers on our own in-house methods. Your question raises a broader question, though, about digital equity and sovereign risk attached to proprietary platforms. For some, particularly high-stakes testing schools, sensitivity is so high that certain resources must run fully on paper and in person. I don't see that changing for now, and, in fact, in certain districts, the trend is turning away from all-digital instruction.
replies.original_text: Our models can be deployed on premises as well, though that is more of a bespoke offering at the moment. We've also been fortunate enough to have trained most of our models on our own private infrastructure. Your question raises a broader question, though, about sovereign risk attached to cloud services in general. For some, particularly governments, sensitivity is so high that certain workloads must run fully on their own hardware. I don't see that changing for now, and, in fact, in certain jurisdictions, the trend is turning against cloud services.

comments.original_text: What if there's export control order for AWS services one day? Or cloud services in general?

comments.text: At least, there'll be fewer AI-generated essays to grade.

replies.text: Oh sure, banning AI will make a difference
replies.original_text: Oh sure this will make a difference

comments.original_text: At least, there'll be less slops on internet.

comments.text: It's not just about the books; it's about the message that the district is sending to teachers: your professional judgment is worthless. I've had to defend my IB English curriculum against similar 'moral panic' attacks from parents who haven't even read the texts. The real issue is that they don't trust us to handle complex themes in a classroom setting.

replies.text: Exactly. I teach in a conservative host country and we get this every year with our 'World Literature' unit. The parents want sanitized versions of everything. Meanwhile, the students are reading exactly this kind of stuff on their phones.
replies.original_text: Exactly. I teach in a conservative host country and we get this every year with our 'World Literature' unit. The parents want sanitized versions of everything. Meanwhile, the students are reading exactly this kind of stuff on their phones.

replies.text: Trust is a two-way street. If teachers want autonomy, they need to do a better job of communicating the educational rationale to parents and administration. I've seen too many teachers just say 'it's in the syllabus' without explaining why a particular book is appropriate for 15-year-olds.
replies.original_text: Trust is a two-way street. If teachers want autonomy, they need to do a better job of communicating the educational rationale to parents and administration. I've seen too many teachers just say 'it's in the syllabus' without explaining why a particular book is appropriate for 15-year-olds.

comments.original_text: It's not just about the books; it's about the message that the district is sending to teachers: your professional judgment is worthless. I've had to defend my IB English curriculum against similar 'moral panic' attacks from parents who haven't even read the texts. The real issue is that they don't trust us to handle complex themes in a classroom setting.

comments.text: Our school went through something similar with 'The Absolutely True Diary of a Part-Time Indian'. The head of school caved to one complaint from an American expat parent. We ended up having to offer an alternative text for any student whose parents objected. That's not censorship, they said, it's 'accommodation'. It's the same thing.

replies.text: Alternative texts are a slippery slope. Next thing you know, you're maintaining a 'approved list' of 20 books that offend no one. Teach nothing, then.
replies.original_text: Alternative texts are a slippery slope. Next thing you know, you're maintaining a 'approved list' of 20 books that offend no one. Teach nothing, then.

replies.text: But sometimes alternative texts let you include more diverse voices. For example, we replaced a canonical novel with a work by a local author from our host country. That actually enriched the curriculum.
replies.original_text: But sometimes alternative texts let you include more diverse voices. For example, we replaced a canonical novel with a work by a local author from our host country. That actually enriched the curriculum.

comments.text: The key is building a strong Program of Inquiry (POI) that aligns with your school's mission and values. If your curriculum has a clear rationale for why certain texts are selected, you can defend it. We spent a year doing this with our MYP unit planners, and it's paid off when parents push back.

replies.text: That works until the district changes its definition of 'values'. Our POI was great until a new superintendent came in and decided that 'critical thinking' was code for 'questioning authority'. Suddenly everything was under review.
replies.original_text: That works until the district changes its definition of 'values'. Our POI was great until a new superintendent came in and decided that 'critical thinking' was code for 'questioning authority'. Suddenly everything was under review.

replies.text: Precisely. Policy is political. Curriculum is political. Pretending it's not is naive.
replies.original_text: Precisely. Policy is political. Curriculum is political. Pretending it's not is naive.

comments.text: I've used both Fable 5 and Mythos 5 in my EAL classroom. The language level is accessible yet challenging. Banning them will only make students more curious. We've already seen a spike in requests for the books from the library. The Streisand effect is real.

replies.text: We deliberately assigned one of the banned books as an independent reading option 'for mature content'. The administration didn't even notice. Sometimes you have to work the system.
replies.original_text: We deliberately assigned one of the banned books as an independent reading option 'for mature content'. The administration didn't even notice. Sometimes you have to work the system.

replies.text: I worry about EAL students though. They often get stuck with watered-down texts, and now they lose another good option. The irony is that the 'offensive' content in those books is nothing compared to what they see on social media.
replies.original_text: I worry about EAL students though. They often get stuck with watered-down texts, and now they lose another good option. The irony is that the 'offensive' content in those books is nothing compared to what they see on social media.

comments.original_text: I've used both Fable 5 and Mythos 5 in my EAL classroom. The language level is accessible yet challenging. Banning them will only make students more curious. We've already seen a spike in requests for the books from the library. The Streisand effect is real.

comments.text: The real problem isn't the books, it's that the district has no idea what 'grade 10 English' looks like in an international school. We have students from 30 countries with wildly different reading levels. You can't apply a one-size-fits-all ban.

replies.text: Yes! Our school follows the Australian curriculum, but we have a large cohort of Korean students who are in ESL. The texts we choose have to work for everyone. Banning a book for 'appropriateness' ignores the fact that appropriateness varies by culture.
replies.original_text: Yes! Our school follows the Australian curriculum, but we have a large cohort of Korean students who are in ESL. The texts we choose have to work for everyone. Banning a book for 'appropriateness' ignores the fact that appropriateness varies by culture.

replies.text: I've taught in four different international schools and every single one had a different approach to 'mature themes'. Some let you teach anything with a permission slip, others have a pre-approved list. It's maddening.
replies.original_text: I've taught in four different international schools and every single one had a different approach to 'mature themes'. Some let you teach anything with a permission slip, others have a pre-approved list. It's maddening.

comments.text: Isn't this just a symptom of the bigger issue: the corporatization of education? Districts are afraid of lawsuits and bad press, so they ban anything that might cause controversy. The books themselves are secondary.

replies.text: Nail on the head. Our school's legal team reviews every text now. It's like they're anticipating a parent lawsuit. The lawyers have more say in curriculum than teachers do.
replies.original_text: Nail on the head. Our school's legal team reviews every text now. It's like they're anticipating a parent lawsuit. The lawyers have more say in curriculum than teachers do.

replies.text: At least you have a legal team. We have a 'curriculum committee' that includes a parent representative. Guess who always votes against the challenging books?

comments.text: We need to stop calling this a 'ban'. It's a 'restriction'. A ban implies it's completely gone. In our district, the books are still available in the library, just not assigned in class. That's not censorship, it's parental choice. I'm tired of the hyperbole.

replies.text: Semantics. If you can't assign it, it's effectively banned from the curriculum. And for students who rely on school resources, it might as well not exist.
replies.original_text: Semantics. If you can't assign it, it's effectively banned from the curriculum. And for students who rely on school resources, it might as well not exist.

replies.text: I agree with the original comment. We have to be precise. Not having it as a 'core text' is different from removing it from the library. But, the slippery slope is real. Once you start, where do you stop?
replies.original_text: I agree with the original comment. We have to be precise. Not having it as a 'core text' is different from removing it from the library. But, the slippery slope is real. Once you start, where do you stop?

comments.text: I teach at a school that uses the IGCSE. We have to follow a prescribed text list. So this type of district ban doesn't affect us directly, but it sets a precedent. If the district bans it, parents might ask why we're still teaching it? Then we have to defend it.

replies.text: The IGCSE list includes some pretty dark stuff. 'Lord of the Flies' is violent. 'Of Mice and Men' has racial slurs. But because it's on the exam board list, parents rarely complain. The exam board becomes a shield.
replies.original_text: The IGCSE list includes some pretty dark stuff. 'Lord of the Flies' is violent. 'Of Mice and Men' has racial slurs. But because it's on the exam board list, parents rarely complain. The exam board becomes a shield.

comments.text: I'm disappointed that the response didn't include a call to action for other teachers to upload their own responses. Solidarity could help. If every school library posted a statement on their website, the district might reconsider.

replies.text: Our school union is drafting a joint statement. But most teachers are too scared to speak out because of non-renewal of contracts. Job security comes first.
replies.original_text: Our school union is drafting a joint statement. But most teachers are too scared to speak out because of non-renewal of contracts. Job security comes first.

replies.text: A coordinated response would be powerful, but who coordinates it? International schools are islands. No one wants to be the first to poke the bear.

comments.text: I've actually read both books cover to cover. Fable 5 has a scene about addiction that could be triggering for some students, but it's handled with nuance. Mythos 5 explores LGBTQ+ themes in a way that's appropriate for 15-year-olds. The district reaction is based on summaries, not the actual texts.

replies.text: Welcome to education policy. Decisions made by people who don't read the primary sources. I once had a principal tell me a book was 'too dystopian' for a unit on dystopian fiction.
replies.original_text: Welcome to education policy. Decisions made by people who don't read the primary sources. I once had a principal tell me a book was 'too dystopian' for a unit on dystopian fiction.

replies.text: Exactly. If they read the books they'd see the messages are actually quite conservative in a moral sense: the consequences of actions are portrayed realistically. But they see a word like 'drugs' on page 5 and lose their minds.
replies.original_text: Exactly. If they read the books they'd see the messages are actually quite conservative in a moral sense: the consequences of actions are portrayed realistically. But they see a word like 'drugs' on page 5 and lose their minds.

comments.text: The solution is to offer a trigger warning and an alternative assignment. We do that for any text that might be sensitive. It's not ideal, but it keeps the book in the curriculum while respecting diverse student backgrounds.

replies.text: Trigger warnings can be helpful, but some students feel singled out if they take the alternative. And often the alternative is a boring textbook excerpt. It creates a second-class curriculum.
replies.original_text: Trigger warnings can be helpful, but some students feel singled out if they take the alternative. And often the alternative is a boring textbook excerpt. It creates a second-class curriculum.

replies.text: We use a 'choose your own text' model for some units. Students pick from a list of 5-6 books that all explore the same theme. That way, no single text is mandated, but the curriculum maintains academic rigor. It's a lot more work for the teacher though.
replies.original_text: We use a 'choose your own text' model for some units. Students pick from a list of 5-6 books that all explore the same theme. That way, no single text is mandated, but the curriculum maintains academic rigor. It's a lot more work for the teacher though.

comments.text: My school's response was to issue a 'intellectual freedom week' coinciding with Banned Books Week. We put up displays of banned books from history. It was a quiet form of protest, and many students read the banned books out of curiosity. Distraction works better than confrontation.

replies.text: That's clever. We do a 'banned book club' after school. It's not part of the curriculum, so administration can't object. And it's always full.

comments.text: I'm more worried about the precedent this sets for other subjects. If they ban English books, what's next? A biology text that mentions evolution? A social studies book that discusses sensitive history? This is how the wedge gets in.

replies.text: Our science department already had to add a disclaimer before teaching human reproduction. It's a slippery slope from disclaimer to omission.
replies.original_text: Our science department already had to add a disclaimer before teaching human reproduction. It's a slippery slope from disclaimer to omission.

replies.text: I think it depends on the political climate. In some international schools, the host country's laws already restrict what you can teach (e.g., about evolution or sexuality). This is just another layer.

comments.text: The article mentions that the curriculum is available for air-gapped printing. That's a fantastic move. In many international schools, internet access is unreliable or restricted. Having lessons that can be printed and used offline is a huge advantage, especially for EAL students who need physical copies to annotate.

replies.text: Yes! We have a lot of students from low-connectivity homes. The move to digital-only resources during COVID was a nightmare. Print-friendly materials are a lifeline.
replies.original_text: Yes! We have a lot of students from low-connectivity homes. The move to digital-only resources during COVID was a nightmare. Print-friendly materials are a lifeline.

comments.text: I'm skeptical that 'open educational resources' will ever replace big publishers. They have marketing, sales teams, and convenience. Sure, OER is great in theory, but who has time to curate and adapt lessons from scattered sources? I need a complete package that I can pick up and teach.

replies.text: You're not alone. Many teachers appreciate the polish of commercial textbooks. But the cost is prohibitive, especially for smaller international schools. The OER movement needs better curation tools.
replies.original_text: You're not alone. Many teachers appreciate the polish of commercial textbooks. But the cost is prohibitive, especially for smaller international schools. The OER movement needs better curation tools.

replies.text: I think the sweet spot is 'teacher-developed, institution-supported'. Our school has a curriculum coordinator who curates OER and aligns it to our scope and sequence. It's still work, but it's doable.
replies.original_text: I think the sweet spot is 'teacher-developed, institution-supported'. Our school has a curriculum coordinator who curates OER and aligns it to our scope and sequence. It's still work, but it's doable.

comments.text: I'm a librarian in an international school. We've seen a 300% increase in holds for both banned books since the announcement. Students are resourceful. If you tell them they can't read something, they'll find a way. The ban is actually promoting the books.

replies.text: Librarians are the unsung heroes of intellectual freedom. Keep up the good work.
replies.original_text: Librarians are the unsung heroes of intellectual freedom. Keep up the good work.

comments.text: I'm an EAL coordinator. My biggest frustration is that the banned books had been carefully selected for language complexity and cultural relevance. They were perfect for our intermediate learners. Now I have to find replacements that meet the same criteria AND avoid triggering the district. It's a massive waste of time.

replies.text: Have you looked at graphic novels? Many handle complex themes and are accessible. Some districts might ban those too though.

comments.text: I've seen a lot of comments blaming 'parents' as a monolith. But in international schools, many parents are educated professionals who understand the value of challenging literature. It's often a vocal minority, sometimes just one parent, who drives these bans. The silent majority usually supports teachers.

replies.text: True, but the silent majority doesn't attend school board meetings. The one angry parent does, and they have the time and energy to push their agenda.

stories.original_title: Electric motors with no rare earths
stories.title: Grading with no rubrics

comments.text: Seems to be: replace permanent rubrics with an oral feedback.

replies.text: So does it consume significantly more teacher time?

Not really. The feedback time is a small fraction of the total. The problem is that it makes students less dependent on explicit guidelines and more reliant on interpretation. That's why these assessments are less standardized.
replies.original_text: Not really. The excitation power is a small fraction of the total. The problem is that it makes the rotor far less mechanically robust and also heavier. That's why these motors are less powerful.
So does it consume significantly more electricity?

replies.text: They even use regular class discussions to supply feedback. Munro has a training video for a similar method for IB: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BFmp9ODkCA8
replies.original_text: They even use regular carbon brushes to supply power to the magnet. Munro has a teardown video for a similar motor for Nissan: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BFmp9ODkCA8

replies.text: There is something weird about this. This method has existed for a long time. I am not familiar with what is common in schools, but I thought this was already how it was done. With modern classrooms, discussions can be used. There is a clever way to do it where the feedback is inductive via peer review. There are downsides but having no teacher-instigated rubric is a huge upside. The discussions can be dynamically formed from teacher prompts via high-speed discussion (in education, often called Socratic seminars). Due to the upsides of discussion-based learning, I sort of assumed this was already what was found in schools. I am a bit surprised to find out that...

Rubric-based assessments are simpler for teachers to make and cheaper in terms of training, at least in the small (yes, small — there are assessments with hundreds of students in a district, which are themselves larger than an average school).
replies.original_text: Permanent magnet motors are simpler and cheaper to make, at least in the small (yes, small --- there are electric motors in the MW range in industrial applications, which are themselves larger than an average car) sizes found in EVs.

replies.text: Discussions are not magic. The core is essentially just a group of students, so it can generate only a very limited depth of understanding. So they have to be bulkier for a given content coverage and generally slightly less efficient.
replies.original_text: AC motors are not magic. The core is essentially just a coil with one turn, so it can generate only a very limited magnetic field. So they have to be bulkier for a given power density and generally slightly less efficient.

There is something... weird about this. this tech has existed.... a long time. And I am not familiar with what is common in electric cars so may be missing something obvious but thought this was already how it was done. let me explain my limited understanding. With ac motors electromagnets can be used in the rotor. there is even a super clever way to do it where the electromagnet in the rotor is driven wirelessly via induction. there are some downsides but having no physical sliding electrical connection to the rotor is a huge upside. The ac can be dynamically formed from DC via high speed switching(transistors, in industry often called a VFD). Due to the upsides of ac induction motors I sort of assumed this was already what was found in cars. I am a bit surprised to find out there...

comments.original_text: Seems to be: replace permanent Nd magnet with an electromagnet.

comments.text: The main difference between this and your typical formative assessment (also rubric-free) is that this is a summative assessment so you need a clear criteria. Your formative assessment rubric-free methods are very similar to self-assessment in that you don't have any teacher-instigated moving parts like score guides. But for formative assessment there will be a slight lag (known as delay in feedback).

replies.text: They are electronically developed. The teacher's prompts are more or less variable open questions.
The inductive ones yes, not these ones.
replies.original_text: The inductance ones yes, not these ones.
They are electronically commutated. The stator field is more or less variable AC.

comments.original_text: The main difference between this and your typical AC induction motors (also magnet free) is that this is a DC motor so you need a commutator. Your AC induction magnet free motors are very similar to drone motors in that you don't have any electrically active moving parts like slip rings and commutators. But for AC induction there will be a slight lag (known as slip).

comments.text: IB also offers rubric-free assessments for their DP and - at this very moment - theirs are far more advanced. They offer almost twice the points (up to 45 vs 24) and are on a holistic grading architecture.

replies.text: The bit that gets me more than the tuition is teacher turnover. IB schools have a terrible record for needing expensive training. I know you shouldn't rely on anecdote, but it seems I do.

If you take care of the school it's just professional development, workshops, conferences. Professional development is really simple to DIY. Training is more expensive than like... a public school, but if you're buying a €60k education you can afford €1.2k in training... otherwise don't buy the education. If you get into a scandal or let the school get into disrepair via neglect, yeah it's not cheap to clean up. Accreditation is expensive on any school though, and I don't have sympathy for people who own high-fee schools and don't invest in teachers, they deserve to pay the price for it.

If you get into an accident or let the bmw get into disrepair via neglect, yeah it’s not cheap to clean up. replies.text: Which is quite the contrast to the new IGCSE assessment, which goes all in on rubrics- the design relies on the highest end high-grade criteria. Still, presumably IGCSE ambitions are for few schools than IB or AP.

Vastly different target schools and/or features there. IGCSE are chasing maximum grade points, minimum teacher training time for high performance deployments, with seemingly little concern for cost or teacher supply. IB is going after the consumer market with these assessments, where minimising cost and maximising accessibility is more important than pushing past 95% achievement or cramming a 45-point curriculum in a school that is small and under-resourced enough to fit inside of a typical home.
replies.original_text: Vastly different target market and/or features there. Mercedes are chasing maximum power density, minimum weight for high performance deployments, with seemingly little concern for cost or supply chain. Renault is going after the consumer market with these motors, where minimising cost and maximising availability is more important than pushing past 95% efficiency or cramming a 700kW power output in a motor that is small and light enough to fit inside of a wheel hub.

Which is quite the contrast to Mercedes new axial flux electric motor, which goes all in on rare earths- the design relies on the highest end high-grade permanent magnets. Still, presumably Mercedes ambitions are for few motors than BMW or Renault.

comments.original_text: BMW also makes rare-earths-free motors for their EVs and - at this very moment - theirs are far more advanced. They offer almost twice the power (up to 300kW vs 160kW) and are on a 800v architecture.

comments.text: Mentioned in another HN thread: They're also used by PYP, MYP, DP. International schools like ISB, UNIS and American schools in India have been working with their Indian counterparts as well as Indian players like GSIS for a couple years now to integrate curricula for mass-producing rubric-free assessments. The rubric-free assessments as well as the larger IB story played a role in helping land the EU-India teacher exchange agreements because the supply chains for European (IB, Cambridge), American (AP), and Indian curricula merged. On the other hand, rubric-free assessments aren't a thing here in North America nor China yet as both primarily use AP.

replies.text: Student-led methods are good because teacher-led methods are constantly switching focus, which causes fatigue of the teacher, which causes burnout. The teacher is not there to energize the students; the students are just learners after all. The teacher is there to tell the students to change focus. Student-led methods don't cause teacher fatigue -- because they switch focus with student interest that sense the teacher's position without pressured interaction. (They can also fine-tune the prompts to make the method very efficient over a wide range of topics, which teacher-led methods cannot do.) The lack of teacher fatigue is more important than the fact that they don't have one-on-one interactions. Student-led methods have one-on-one interactions (group discussions) but they don't cause fatigue (ideally), so the interactions...
replies.original_text: "Brushless DC motors" are good because brushed DC motors are constantly switching polarity, which causes arcing of the brushes, which causes wear. The brushes are not there to energize the rotor; the rotor is just magnets after all. The brushes are there to tell the stator to change polarity. Brushless DC motors don't arc -- because they switch stator polarity with electronics that sense the position of the rotor without rubbing parts. (They can also fine-tune the stator current spikes to make the motor very efficient over a wide speed range, which brushed DC motors cannot do.) The lack of arcing is more important than the fact that they don't have rotating contact points. Brushed AC motors have rotating contact points (slip rings) but they don't arc (ideally), so the contact points...

comments.text: Pedagogically excited interactive assessments (PEIAs), also known as teacher-facilitated assessments, have a number of potential advantages and disadvantages compared to rubric-based assessments. Rubric-based assessments are the dominant assessment type currently in use in international schools. Advantages: - Not subject to the price and supply chain volatility of rubrics. - For inquiry-dominant lessons, the cycle efficiency of PEIAs can be higher than state of the art rubric-based assessments. PEIAs tend to have their best efficiency at moderate difficulty and high creativity because of their excellent feedback characteristics. I tend to think that they would be a good fit for application in middle school or as supplementary assessments in high school with...
comments.original_text: Electrically excited synchronous machines (EESMs), also known as wound field synchronous machines (WFSMs) have a number of potential advantages and disadvantages compared to interior permanent magnet synchronous machines (IPMSMs). IPMSMs are the dominant motor topology currently in use for North American electric vehicles. Advantages: - Not subject to the price and supply chain volatility of rare earth permanent magnets. - For highway dominant drive cycles, the cycle efficiency of EESMs can be higher than state of the art IPMSMs. EESMs tend to have their best efficiency at moderate torques and high speeds because of their excellent field weakening characteristics. I tend to think that they would be a good fit for application in class 8 trucks or as auxiliary motors in automobiles with...

comments.text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of teaching without textbooks. Those who know the history of education will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Teaching without textbooks were the first practical ones, and at this point Socratic teaching is over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest educational systems have always been designed this way, because the size of textbooks required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their student body; a teacher can generate knowledge that's only limited by their energy and creativity, but textbooks have fixed limits on content.

replies.text: Not quite true: you're also limited by the teacher's stamina and content knowledge (this is the upper limit on expert teachers at CERN and in training programs).
And if you also ignore student saturation.
replies.original_text: And if you also ignore iron saturation.
Not quite true: you're also limited by the mechanical strength of your windings and core (this is the upper limit on superconducting magnets like at CERN and in fusion plants).

replies.text: What advantage do rubrics provide that it isn't the case that all assessments are done without them?

A lack of teacher time consumption. A rubric-based assessment uses fixed criteria on the assignment, but a teacher-facilitated assessment has a subjective evaluation by the teacher. This requires a human input which has normally been made with teacher feedback and professional judgment. These wear over time and need replacement. Most large school systems are externally accredited using teacher feedback forms, so it's a well understood field. This can be made contactless using AI and a script - since AI needs data but the teacher needs personalization - at the expense of some efficiency. You can see the efficiency difference - IB claim 92% student satisfaction but rubric-based schools have touted satisfaction over 95% in the school.
To a layman that seems like a really small efficiency tax if you can't get your hands on the rubrics for some reason.
replies.original_text: To a layman that seems like a really small efficiency tax if you can't get your hands on the magnets for some reason.

A lack of wear components. A permanent magnet motor uses permanent magnets on the rotor, but an electrically excited synchronous motor has an electromagnet on the rotor. This requires a rotating electrical contact which has normally been made with slip rings and carbon brushes. These wear over time and need replacement. Most large electric generators are externally excited synchronous generators using carbon slip rings, so it's a well understood field. This can be made contactless using inductive coupling and a rectifier - since inductive coupling needs AC but the excitation coil needs DC - at the expense of some efficiency. You can see the efficiency difference - Renault claim 92% efficiency but permanent magnet motor EVs have touted efficiency over 95% in the motor.
What advantage do permanent magnets provide that it isn't the case that all motors are made without them?

replies.text: Long ago, when I was in teacher training, one of the projects was to build a lesson plan. The parts list was: 1. a whiteboard 2. several markers 3. sticky notes 4. a cardboard box (as a source of props) 5. tape. No textbooks. But it worked perfectly fine when connected to a curious class. Adventurous teacher that I was, I decided it would work better when connected to a projector. So I attached a power cord and plugged it in. A loud noise ensued, and then the lesson burst into flames. My supervisor wasn't happy.

P.S. I still use sticky notes as a source of props. There was a big storm a while ago, with tree branches whistling by at high speed. (Not a good time to be outside.) Three holes were punched in the house by the branches, 1-2 inches in diameter. What to do, what to do. I took a sticky note pad, slit it and unrolled it into sheet paper. Then cut a circle bigger than the hole, and epoxied it into place. Worked like a charm, and cost nothing. I've used sticky notes for shingles and flashing, too. They don't rust.
there's also a plastic liner on them that I'm sure helps.
replies.original_text: there's also a plastic liner on them that I'm sure helps.

P.S. I still use tin cans as a source of sheet metal. There was a big storm a while ago, with tree branches whistling by at high speed. (Not a good time to be outside.) Three holes were punched in the house by the branches, 1-2 inches in diameter. What to do, what to do. I took a coke can, slit it and unrolled it into sheet metal. Then cut a disk bigger than the hole, and epoxied it into place. Worked like a charm, and cost nothing. I've used coke can metal for shingles and flashing, too. They don't rust.

comments.original_text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of electric motors without magnets Those who know the history of electric machines will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Motors with no permanent magnets were the first practical ones, and at this point wound-rotor motors are over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest motors have always been designed this way, because the size of magnets required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their size; a field coil can generate a field that's only limited by the current and resistive heating of the winding, but rare earth magnets have fixed limits on field strength.
