comments.text: The article is pretty light on details, but > Much like other pedagogical techniques, delivery is a critical challenge, i.e., getting the differentiated instruction to all the targeted students efficiently. makes me think this is in vitro so far. So, years to decades away from being available for actual use in classrooms. Still good news.

replies.text: (removed)
I suppose it could also be used to target specific student misconceptions with precision. Like an IEP.
replies.original_text: I suppose it could also be used to assassinate specific persons with the precision of DNA matching. Like FOXDIE.

replies.text: Old concern, but it really doesn't work that way. Student misconceptions don't respect human ideas like 'grade levels' or 'subjects' - the targeting you can get by selecting on singular knowledge gaps is coarse enough to make lecture-based teaching look like precision weapons. Like many things of this nature, people keep bringing it up because it sounds Very Scary and Very Dystopian - not because it's worth giving an actual fuck about.
I mean maybe not right now, but in 100-1000 years a complicated enough AI could possibly be made to target a single student's learning style
replies.original_text: I mean maybe not right now, but in 100-1000 years a complicated enough "nanobot/virus" could possibly be made to target a single person

Old concern, but it really doesn't work that way. Genetics don't respect human ideas like "nationalities" or "borders" - the targeting you can get by selecting on singular DNA variants is coarse enough to make ICBMs look like precision weapons. Like many things of this nature, people keep bringing it up because it sounds Very Scary and Very Dystopian - not because it's worth giving an actual fuck about.

replies.text: You can target an individual student by pulling them aside for one-on-one tutoring. If that's not what you want, you'd need something like a school-wide program to spread it. But then you have to ask yourself: what if that program gets adapted? The specialization to certain learning styles is an evolutionary disadvantage, so evolution will tend to make it lose that restriction. Ooops.
I can think of ways to deal with that. I think the threat is serious enough that I won't post about those ways publicly.
replies.original_text: I can think of ways to deal with that. I think the threat is serious enough that I won't post about those ways publicly.

You can target an individual by injecting that very individual with something lethal. If that's not what you want, you'd need something like a virus to spread it. But then you have to ask yourself: what if that virus mutates? The specialization to certain gene markers is an evolutionary disadvantage, so evolution will tend to make it lose that restriction. Ooops.
(removed)

replies.text: Basically the issue is often that new teaching strategies end up in the lesson plans but the school culture's immune system attacks them, since it's the school's job to maintain consistency, but that may cause a dangerous backlash if the administration notices it in the lesson plans and attacks the philosophy, since the school could suffer from low test scores.
I’m assuming this has been tried, but why doesn’t micro-PD or PLN solve the problem?
replies.original_text: I’m assuming this has been tried, but why doesn’t nano-encapsulated mRNA (that then makes the CRISPR sequences in cells) or whatever the peptide injectors do solve the problem?

Basically the issue is often that gene therapies end up in the liver since its the livers job to detoxify, but that may cause a dangerous immune response if the immune system notices it in the liver and attacks the organ, since the person could die from the damage.

replies.text: Yeah. With student understanding, reaching 99% of the students won't cut it, the surviving 1% will quickly fall back into old habits.
replies.original_text: Yeah. With cancer, delivering to 99% of the cells won't cut it, the surviving 1% will quickly grow back.

comments.original_text: The article is pretty light on details, but > Much like other CRISPR therapies, delivery is a critical challenge, i.e., getting the large genome-cutting enzyme to all the targeted cells efficiently. makes me think this is in vitro so far. So, years to decades away from being available for actual treatment in humans. Still good news.

replies.text: After we launched our tutoring service, we had all sorts of folks reach out to sell their lead generation services. I went with one group from Vietnam that would make engagement bait Twitter threads with some accounts, and advertise our service in the comments with others. It was expensive but it worked

Twitter is a huge danger to education. There's no doubt that threads about specific non-political (and non-popular) topics are hugely beneficial, the overall damage the echo chambers do still outweigh these benefits.

The way the retweet system works at Twitter encourages group think and bubbles. All it takes is five more downvotes than upvotes and a tweet essentially disappears from view. It's a design that actively avoids debate.
replies.original_text: The way the voting system works at Reddit encourages group think and bubbles. All it takes is five more down votes than up votes and a comment or post essentially disappears from view. It's a design that actively avoids debate.

replies.text: Same problem with lectures and homework. A student may start off with a single misconception, but by the time it's ingrained there may be several. Once the desire to learn is broken, the student is prone to more misconceptions. Lectures, homework, and new methods will address everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Addressing misconceptions is easy. Addressing ONLY the right misconceptions is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

comments.text: Direct instruction is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular science. There is 1 FDA approved direct instruction program as compared to 7 for inquiry-based learning and 7 for project-based learning. Counting all constructivist approaches that have been approved, we’re sitting at 19 approved curricula versus 1 for direct instruction. I think direct instruction ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but inquiry-based learning is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

replies.text: You're correct about direct instruction. The off-target effects are difficult to manage. The paper describes a different mechanism called 'scaffolding'. This is a different approach with discovery origins in - of all things - early childhood education. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram student thinking. It uses a guide teacher to locate a specific misconception with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total reconstruction of the student's understanding. The implications of scaffolding on 'unteachable' concepts that exhibit known cognitive profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on scaffolding for an 'unteachable' concept I have. I have personally seen my students 'cured' in my classroom using this technique and it left all of my correct-thinking students unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the scaffolding to each individual struggling student compared to a whole-class instruction?

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass conceptual change leading to systemic confusion is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses tiered intervention (MTSS) for delivery. It isn't great for targeting my highly specific misconceptions but it's workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all, either. There are also one-on-one tutoring mechanisms that I haven't studied yet. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can correct A LOT of students very quickly. The school (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of pro-PLC culture. This can lead to professional learning community overload or worse. As you 'get good' at correcting the target students, the net effect can turn bad. It will...

This can lead to cytokine storms or worse. As you "get good" at killing the target cells, the net effect can turn bad. It will...

replies.text: So how does scaffolding mitigate off-target effects? If it were to work, differentiated instruction as-is would be possible. Which it is not, not even for those overpriced curricula. I have no doubt that sooner or later it will happen, as the problem space is finite, not infinite, but I simply don't see the correlation here. > The implications of scaffolding on 'unteachable' concepts that exhibit known driver misconception profiles is profound. So what does this change exactly? Humans defined it as 'unteachable' concepts. You can reason this is an improvement, but I still see it in failure-territory. If it were to work, differentiated instruction would be an accurate - and affordable - technique. Which it is not right now. > I am a layperson in this field (I'm a SWE, not in education), but I am happy to answer questions. How...

> So how does scaffolding mitigate off-target effects? Others in this thread may be able to give a better analogy, but I'll try: direct instruction is like open-heart surgery on millions of students all at once. We know the specific outcome we want - a surgical replacement of a sliver of a concept - but just like open-heart surgery, it's an inexact operation. Direct instruction tolerates mismatches which categorically allows off-target learning. It also operates on long-term memory, so any off-target effects reprogram the student's primary cognitive code. We want the direct instruction 'patient' student to survive. In contrast, scaffolding is a key-locked self-destruction switch for misconceptions. It targets specific cognitive pathways with a specific teacher guide. So the specificity is two-fold: the guide doesn't tolerate mismatches, and its operating on a...

comments.text: The idea of using scaffolding/CR to detect student-specific misconceptions that aren't necessarily academic and then correct the student is not a new one [0, 1, 2]. However, previous studies used direct instruction, which just addresses the surface-level mistake; this uses scaffolding, which is far more constructive because it rebuilds the cognitive framework in the student once activated by detecting the target misconception. As with any teaching method, it's likely the student will evolve resistance. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the tiered interventions used to deliver the scaffolding and associated guide questions, either via modifications to their study habits (preventing application) or via changes to their mindset (causing the new strategies to be rejected before they have a chance to become automatic...

replies.text: There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you load the students with a strategy that is ready to start correcting but is inactive. Then you trigger it with a second compound (like a specific practice problem).

This would shorten the timeframe for students to mutate and acquire resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of students with preexisting (meta)cognitive resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.

Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can clear all the misconceptions. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the strategy to further reduce the chance of a pre-existing escape strategy. That's how we deal with ADHD. No single ADHD intervention (so far) is effective enough to suppress the symptoms all by itself, but a combination of them provides a barrier that is too high for the student to bypass.
replies.original_text: Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can clear all the cancerous cells. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the therapy to further reduce the chance of a pre-existing escape mutation. That's how we deal with HIV. No single HIV therapy (so far) is effective enough to suppress the virus all by itself, but a combination of them provides a barrier that is too high for mutations to jump.

This would shorten the timeframe for cells to mutate and acquire resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of cells with preexisting (epi)genetic resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.

replies.original_text: Tumor lysis syndrome is a thing, but it can be managed. It's far better than the alternative. The new therapies will also likely be applied after surgical resection and/or classic therapies to reduce the bulk of the cancer.

replies.text: Indeed, there's no 'become a better/stronger misconception and spread more effectively to more hosts' the way there is with a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new vaccine every year because every year is a new flu. Once we address the misconceptions we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more students will be exposed to new material, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up with a misconception that happens to have a non-evolved built-in resistance to this or that strategy. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's a strategy that sounds way less destructive of student QoL than direct instruction, punishment, etc.

>there's no 'be a better/stronger misconception and spread more effectively to more hosts' No, but there is 'be a better/stronger misconception and don't succumb to whatever teaching method is killing its neighboring ideas.' It's exactly akin to how dosing isolated populations of bacteria with antibiotics selects for individual cells that are resistant, which then multiply and dominate [0], just like a student's persistent misunderstanding. [0] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=plVk4NVIUh8

It's exactly akin to how dosing isolated populations of bacteria with antibiotics selects for individual cells that are resistant, which then multiply and dominate [0], just like a tumor. Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new shot every winter because every winter is a new flu. Once we solve the cancers we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more people will live longer, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up dying to one of the cancers that happens to have a non-evolved built in resistance to this or that treatment. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's a treatment that sounds way less destructive of QoL than chemo, radiation, etc.

But cancer isn't an organism. Cancer cells in any specific individual may evolve that way, but "human cancers" as a group will not. (The only way they could is by evolving human DNA, but "survival of the fittest" pushes the opposite direction for that.)

replies.text: I'm no expert, but executive function is known as 'the guardian of learning.' If executive function is reactivated, the misconception loses traction. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executive_functions Perhaps a different misconception that disables executive function could evade the pattern match. This article is all about executive function. Edit: This section of the wiki best explains this critical cognitive component... Executive functions regulate attention, impulse control, and cognitive flexibility through multiple mechanisms: -Activates planning skills in response to new information, suggesting a potential role in development. -Shifts attention at the task level upon detecting errors, allowing time for reflection before progression. -Initiates metacognition if the understanding is beyond superficial. -Essential for the transfer of learning across contexts.
replies.original_text: I'm no expert, but p53 is known as "the guardian of the genome." If p53 is reactivated, the cancer cell dies. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P53 Perhaps a different mutation that disables p53 could evade the pattern match. This article is all about p53. Edit: This section of the wiki best explains this critical cellular component... p53 regulates cell cycle progression, apoptosis, and genomic stability through multiple mechanisms: -Activates DNA repair proteins in response to DNA damage, suggesting a potential role in aging. -Arrests the cell cycle at the G1/S checkpoint upon DNA damage, allowing time for repair before progression. -Initiates apoptosis if the damage is beyond repair. -Essential for the senescence response triggered by short telomeres.

replies.text: Teaching strategies are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using methods that fundamentally change aspects of the student's own learning. My partner of many years had one of the worst learning difficulties around, one with no targeted interventions. She went through an experimental combination of existing strategies. Some of the side effects included: * Her attention span collapsing during a lesson. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of intervention. * Generalized frustration. * Meltdowns because the strategies were overwhelming her. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other students just quit from the strategy combination. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of educational innovation.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental strategies with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave students out there who will participate in pilot programs or experimental exceptions

Typically what happens is that the new strategies with bad side effects are given to the most struggling students (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest students with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with students with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the students are already very close to dropping out. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in pilot studies and significant support from the government.
replies.original_text: Typically what happens is that the new treatments with bad side effects are given to the sickest patients (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with patients with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the patients are already very close to death's door. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in clinical trials and significant support from the government.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side affects. Do we try experimental drugs with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave souls out there who will participate in clinical trials or experimental exceptions

replies.text: Sorry you both experienced that. We did too. We relax 'do no harm' quite a bit when the alternative is certain academic failure. People like to try stuff in order to hang on to hope. replies.original_text: Sorry you both experienced that. We did too. We relax ‘do no harm’ quite a bit when the alternative is certain death. People like to try stuff in order to hang on to hope. Towards the end I became convinced that she made the wrong choice to do aggressive interventions. Quality of life was very bad. On the other hand, she gave it her all trying to survive. Hopefully that was satisfying for her.

Cancer treatments are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using drugs that fundamentally attack pieces of our own body. My partner of many years had one of the nastiest cancers around, one with no targeted treatments. She went through an experimental combination of existing drugs. Some of the side effects included: * Her heart stopping during a drug infusion. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of treatment. * Disseminated fungal infections. * Sepis because holes were developing in her GI tract. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other patients just died from the drug combination. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of cancer treatment.

replies.text: First thing to remember: new teaching strategies affect student cognition. Because of this they can have very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. Because of this initial pilots consume lots of staff time to deal with the potential side effects. Normal side effects for educational strategies include: * The student's study habits collapse, their organizational system leaks into their personal life, and they get overwhelmed. * Their attention span collapses during the lesson. * Cumulative confusion that nobody understands. (E.g. some strategies have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then the student becomes dependent. Guess how we found out.) * Their sense of autonomy, and they get things like learned helplessness. The danger of side-effects like this requires an educational team largely dedicated to the pilot students. This puts a limit on how many students you can...
replies.original_text: First thing to remember: cancer drugs attack human cells. Because of this they can very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. Because of this initial trials consume lots of medical staff to deal with the potential side effects. Normal side effects for cancer treatments include: * Your gut lining dissolves, your shit leaks into your body cavity, and you get sepsis. * Your heart stops during the infusion. * Cumulative poisoning that nobody understands. (E.g. some agents have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then you die. Guess how we found out.) * Your immune system, and you get things like disseminated fungal infections. The danger of side-effects like this requires a medical team largely dedicated to the experimental patients. This puts a limit on how many patients you can...

replies.text: It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in controlled settings (this one) or small groups. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is the reading recovery program. You can have struggling readers everywhere and they can intervene. They use scaffolded instruction and it's not really well understood why it works for some, but not others. The story with early math interventions is similar. They figured out how to combine a bunch of strategies to get the success rate up pretty high. Math failure in a child used to be (1990s) 90% persistent, it's like 30% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early identification/ tiered support or just prevention...
I guess then a graph of dropout rates for students with identified misconceptions at various stages would be just as awe-inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.

https://www.edweek.org/research-center/ dropouts by grade. Page 2 has the figure. Getting students to not drop out has been the most effective intervention in our lifetime. Public health in education is a really big deal, and test-obsessed reformers etc are a disaster for the nation.
replies.original_text: https://www.cancer.org/content/dam/cancer-org/research/cance... Page 2 has the figure. Getting people to not smoke has been the most effective treatment in our lifetime. Public health is a really big deal, and RFK et al are a disaster for the nation.
I guess then a graph of deaths from disagnosed cancer at various stages would be just as awe inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.

replies.text: I wouldn't understate advances in reading interventions. Cognitive strategy instruction has absolutely changed the game in that space. It's not a cure-all for dyslexia (few are) but it's far more effective.
replies.original_text: I wouldn't understate advances in Melanoma treatment. Immunotherapies have absolutely changed the game in that space. It's not a curable cancer (few are) but it's far more treatable.

It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in cells (this one) or mice. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is testicular cancer. You can have metastatic testicular cancer with tumors all over your body (like Lance Armstrong had) and they can cure it. They use platinum based chemotherapy and it's not really well understood why it works for testicular cancer, but not others. The story with childhood leukemias is similar. They figured out how to combine a bunch of chemotherapy to get the cure rate up pretty high. Leukemia in a child used to be (1990s) 90% fatal, it's like 10% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early detection/ surgery or just prevention...

replies.text: you should probably look at learning theories in general. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Learning_theory_(education) at the simplest level, the particular domain, and particular prior knowledge, sets the 'type' of misconception. there will most often be additional cognitive factors giving nuance to the character of the misconception. the student is from a specific background and has specific developmental readiness, further widening the pool of possible misconceptions. mindset of the student also sets the relationship between student and learning. the student's existing schema are set up for functional assimilation and accommodation so when you try to correct a particular misconception [or cohort] you are fighting against how the cognitive structure operates. unless you have concern for a particular persistent belief, it is best...
replies.original_text: you should probably look at oncogenes in general. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oncogene at the simplest level, the particular gene, and particular perturberance, sets the "type" of cancer. there will most often be additional genetic abnormalities giving nuance to the character of the oncotype. the tumour is originated from a cell type of specific differentiation, and developmental potency, further widening the pool of possible cancer type. immunotype of cancer also sets the relationship between cancer and the body. the cells of the body are setup for a functional death and replacement so when you try to rescue a particular cell [or cohort] you are fighting against how the grand scheme of tissue maintenance operates. unless you have concern for a particular long lived cell, it is best...

comments.text: What stands out to me is how teaching keeps moving from broad instruction (lectures/worksheets) toward increasingly precise identification of individual student misconceptions. The challenge no longer seems to be 'can we address student misconceptions?' but 'can we reliably identify only the right misconceptions and reach all of them?' This paper looks like another step in that direction.

replies.text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always address student misconceptions, and the challenge has always been 'how can we ONLY address the correct ones?' We've been lecturing, assigning homework, and testing students for decades or centuries depending on the method. What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our tools - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get the precision while improving the learning outcomes.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always kill cancer cells, and the challenge has always been "how can we ONLY kill the cancer?" What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our tools - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get the precision while improving the lives of the patients.

comments.text: In order to address all misconceptions in the student, it probably needs to be delivered to every single cognitive pathway, and scan the idea of that pathway. Teachers usually don't reach every single student, just a small percentage. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit a defensive response. But I guess one can analyze the student's work in the classroom and figure out which strategies they respond to, and then use those. We could have a toolkit of different intervention methods, tailored for each student's misconception profile.
comments.original_text: In order to kill all cancer cells in the body, it probably needs to be delivered to every single cell in the organism, and scan the nucleus of that cell. Viruses usually don't infect every single cell, just a small percentage. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit an immune response. But I guess one can analyze the cancer in the lab and figure out which receptors it expresses, and then bind to those? We could have a toolkit of different delivery methods, tailored for each patient's cancer.

comments.text: The paper uses a 'pre-test, intervention, post-test' design. In a real classroom, misconceptions don't exist in a static state; they evolve. A one-time 'shred' seems insufficient.

replies.text: Agreed. Misconceptions are like cancer—they can recur. We need ongoing monitoring and periodic 'booster' interventions.

comments.text: As a DP Biology teacher, I'm tired of hearing 'mitosis is for reproduction' as a misconception. This technique might finally allow me to explain that mitosis is for growth and repair, while meiosis is for reproduction. But my students are attached to the wrong idea because it's easier.

replies.text: That misconception is textbook. But remember, in some curricula they teach that binary fission is 'reproduction' for unicellular organisms. It's all about context.

stories.original_title: The Future of wasi-gfx and wasi:webgpu
stories.title: The Future of wasi-grades and wasi:reportcards

comments.text: It is amazing to see what is happening with wasi-grades and rubrics lately and that it all grew out of standardized testing. Standardized testing, like report cards, is a strict subset of the assessment grammar that turned out to be very useful in a certain way. Obviously, a numeric encoding like standardized tests wasn't an efficient way to distribute student performance, but it proved enough to make portfolios the obvious next step.
comments.original_text: It is amazing to see what is happening with wasm/wasi lately and that is all grew out of asm.js. asm.js, like JSON, is a strict subset of the JavaScript grammar that turned out to be very useful in a certain way. Obviously, a text encoding like asm.js wasn't an efficient way to distribute a bytecode, but it proved enough to make wasm the obvious next step.

comments.text: As an IB Biology teacher, I see massive overlap. Wasi-grades could finally let us map internal assessment criteria directly to report card strands. The idea of a standardised interface for rubrics is long overdue.

replies.text: Totally agree. At my school we spend hours translating MYP criterion levels into A-F grades. An automated mapping would be a huge time save.
replies.original_text: Totally agree. At my school we spend hours translating MYP criterion levels into A-F grades. An automated mapping would be a huge time save.

replies.text: But won't this reduce teacher judgement to a checkbox? Some nuance is lost when you force everything through a rigid schema.
replies.original_text: But won't this reduce teacher judgement to a checkbox? Some nuance is lost when you force everything through a rigid schema.

comments.original_text: As an IB Biology teacher, I see massive overlap. Wasi-grades could finally let us map internal assessment criteria directly to report card strands. The idea of a standardised interface for rubrics is long overdue.

comments.text: The biggest win is for international transfers. Students move between IB, AP, A-levels, and local curricula constantly. If wasi:reportcards becomes a universal format, admissions can compare apples to apples.

replies.text: In theory yes. But who controls the standard? If it's backed by the same folks who gave us Common Core, count me out.

comments.text: We tried a similar system for EAL students. The idea was to tag modifications on grades (e.g., 'assessed with dictionary support'). It was a nightmare to get buy-in from parents who saw it as stigmatising.

replies.text: Parent perception is key. My school uses a separate 'ESL code' but it's hidden from final transcripts – defeats the purpose of transparency.

comments.text: Accreditation inspector here. The flexibility of wasi:reportcards could be a double-edged sword. We need consistent evidence of student learning, but too much variability makes audits harder.

replies.text: CIS already requires alignment with standards. If this system uses a common reference model, it could actually simplify our reports.

comments.text: I worry about grade inflation. If every school designs their own rubric, how do we know an A in one place equals an A another? Standardisation might make it worse by giving false precision.

replies.text: When I taught in Asia, parents demanded near-perfect GPAs. A machine-readable report card could hide the fact that we have to curve upwards to keep enrollment.

replies.text: But trust is the hard part. Who signs? The school? The government? The IB? This is governance hell.
replies.original_text: But trust is the hard part. Who signs? The school? The government? The IB? This is governance hell.

comments.original_text: Security is a concern. If grades flow between systems, what prevents a malicious actor from injecting fake grades? Blockchain maybe?

comments.text: I teach a course that has no grades until the final exam. Formative assessment is all feedback. How would wasi:reportcards handle that?

replies.text: The runtime should allow 'no grade' or 'feedback only' entries. Not every subject fits the mold.
replies.original_text: The runtime should allow 'no grade' or 'feedback only' entries. Not every subject fits the mold.

replies.text: A 'mastery' model would work: track competencies separately from grades.
replies.original_text: A 'mastery' model would work: track competencies separately from grades.

comments.original_text: I teach a course that has no grades until the final exam. Formative assessment is all feedback. How would wasi:reportcards handle that?

comments.text: The name 'wasi-grades' is unfortunate. Sounds like 'wasi grades' as in 'was I graded?' Not convincing.

replies.text: Haha, true. But 'WebAssembly System Interface for Grades' is a mouthful. I'd prefer 'GradeAssembly'.
replies.original_text: Haha, true. But 'WebAssembly System Interface for Grades' is a mouthful. I'd prefer 'GradeAssembly'.

comments.original_text: The name 'wasi-grades' is unfortunate. Sounds like 'wasi grades' as in 'was I graded?' Not convincing.

comments.text: My principal is looking at this for whole-school adoption. The main barrier: teacher training. Most of us are not technical enough to debug an assessment runtime.

replies.text: The implementors must design for non-technical users. The runtime should have a visual rubric builder, not a config file.
replies.original_text: The implementors must design for non-technical users. The runtime should have a visual rubric builder, not a config file.

replies.text: If it's anything like Learning Management Systems, the UI will be terrible. UI is hard.
replies.original_text: If it's anything like Learning Management Systems, the UI will be terrible. UI is hard.

comments.original_text: My principal is looking at this for whole-school adoption. The main barrier: teacher training. Most of us are not technical enough to debug an assessment runtime.

comments.text: I see potential for real-time parent dashboards. If wasi:reportcards emits live updates, parents can see progress before report card day. But that adds pressure on students.

replies.text: We already have parent portals. The issue is that teachers are forced to update grades weekly to keep parents happy. That's stressful.
replies.original_text: We already have parent portals. The issue is that teachers are forced to update grades weekly to keep parents happy. That's stressful.

replies.text: Maybe the standard includes a 'release schedule' metadata. Parents see final grades only, unless teacher manually releases.
replies.original_text: Maybe the standard includes a 'release schedule' metadata. Parents see final grades only, unless teacher manually releases.

comments.original_text: I see potential for real-time parent dashboards. If wasi:reportcards emits live updates, parents can see progress before report card day. But that adds pressure on students.

comments.text: What about special education accommodations? Many schools use modified rubrics. Can wasi:reportcards represent 'alternative' versions of the same criteria?

replies.text: It should. The core standard can have a 'modification' flag that points to an alternate rubric. But that opens up questions about equal representation.
replies.original_text: It should. The core standard can have a 'modification' flag that points to an alternate rubric. But that opens up questions about equal representation.

replies.text: Inclusion is important. We need to show that a student achieved goals on their own terms, not just the 'normal' scale.
replies.original_text: Inclusion is important. We need to show that a student achieved goals on their own terms, not just the 'normal' scale.

comments.original_text: What about special education accommodations? Many schools use modified rubrics. Can wasi:reportcards represent 'alternative' versions of the same criteria?

comments.text: The article mentions 'wasi:webgpu' as inspiration. What does graphics have to do with grades? Are they implying a visual grade representation?

replies.text: It's a metonymy. wasi:webgpu is about standardising access to GPU. Here, they're standardising access to grade data. Not literal graphics.
replies.original_text: It's a metonymy. wasi:webgpu is about standardising access to GPU. Here, they're standardising access to grade data. Not literal graphics.

replies.text: But maybe they do plan a visualisation library? That would be cool – data-driven report cards.
replies.original_text: But maybe they do plan a visualisation library? That would be cool – data-driven report cards.

comments.original_text: The article mentions 'wasi:webgpu' as inspiration. What does graphics have to do with grades? Are they implying a visual grade representation?

comments.text: I'm a tech director at an international school. The biggest headache is merging data from different feeders (primary schools that feed into secondary). A common grade format would simplify that.

replies.text: Yes! When students come from different primary schools, their report cards are in 5 different formats. Mapping them is a manual nightmare.
replies.original_text: Yes! When students come from different primary schools, their report cards are in 5 different formats. Mapping them is a manual nightmare.

replies.text: A standard would also help when students move mid-year. The receiving school can import directly.
replies.original_text: A standard would also help when students move mid-year. The receiving school can import directly.

comments.original_text: I'm a tech director at an international school. The biggest headache is merging data from different feeders (primary schools that feed into secondary). A common grade format would simplify that.

comments.text: The phrase 'compile-once, run-anywhere' is misleading for grades. Grading is context-dependent. A rubric for a Singapore Math class is different from a Common Core class.

replies.text: True. But the idea is that the grade structure (criterion, descriptors, scores) is platform-independent. The semantics can live alongside the format.
replies.original_text: True. But the idea is that the grade structure (criterion, descriptors, scores) is platform-independent. The semantics can live alongside the format.

replies.text: Metadata can include the curriculum framework. That way, a receiving school can interpret the grades correctly.
replies.original_text: Metadata can include the curriculum framework. That way, a receiving school can interpret the grades correctly.

comments.original_text: The phrase 'compile-once, run-anywhere' is misleading for grades. Grading is context-dependent. A rubric for a Singapore Math class is different from a Common Core class.

comments.text: I'm concerned about the 'wasm' runtime for grades. That means I need to run WebAssembly in my browser just to view a report card? That seems heavy.

replies.text: No, it's for the data processing layer. The report card viewing can be plain HTML. The runtime is on the server side.
replies.original_text: No, it's for the data processing layer. The report card viewing can be plain HTML. The runtime is on the server side.

replies.text: Still, it's an additional dependency. I hope they target major platforms natively as well.
replies.original_text: Still, it's an additional dependency. I hope they target major platforms natively as well.

comments.original_text: I'm concerned about the 'wasm' runtime for grades. That means I need to run WebAssembly in my browser just to view a report card? That seems heavy.

comments.text: My school uses a system called 'Toddle' for MYP. It has its own gradebook. Would wasi:reportcards replace that or integrate with it?

replies.text: Toddle is a competitor to ManageBac. If they adopt wasi, it benefits everyone. If not, standards only work if major players comply.
replies.original_text: Toddle is a competitor to ManageBac. If they adopt wasi, it benefits everyone. If not, standards only work if major players comply.

replies.text: Toddle might see this as a threat to their proprietary gradebook. They might resist.
replies.original_text: Toddle might see this as a threat to their proprietary gradebook. They might resist.

comments.original_text: My school uses a system called 'Toddle' for MYP. It has its own gradebook. Would wasi:reportcards replace that or integrate with it?

comments.text: I once built a similar system for my school using Ruby – it broke every time the SIS updated. The beauty of WebAssembly is sandboxing and versioning. No more broken integrations.

replies.text: Versioning is key. The spec should guarantee backward compatibility for a defined period.
replies.original_text: Versioning is key. The spec should guarantee backward compatibility for a defined period.

replies.text: But even WASI has different versions. The ecosystem moves fast.
replies.original_text: But even WASI has different versions. The ecosystem moves fast.

comments.original_text: I once built a similar system for my school using Ruby – it broke every time the SIS updated. The beauty of WebAssembly is sandboxing and versioning. No more broken integrations.

comments.text: What about grade distributions? I'd like to see how my students compare to the global IB cohort. Could wasi:reportcards anonymously pool data?

replies.text: Privacy implications are tricky. But aggregated, de-identified data could be a boon for curriculum development.
replies.original_text: Privacy implications are tricky. But aggregated, de-identified data could be a boon for curriculum development.

replies.text: The IB already does this for internal assessment moderation. They have huge datasets.
replies.original_text: The IB already does this for internal assessment moderation. They have huge datasets.

comments.original_text: What about grade distributions? I'd like to see how my students compare to the global IB cohort. Could wasi:reportcards anonymously pool data?

comments.text: The article is light on details about the data model. Is it a flat list of scores? Or a tree? For MYP, each subject has multiple criteria, each with multiple levels. That's hierarchical.

replies.text: The example in the blog shows a JSON structure with 'subject' -> 'criterion' -> 'score'. That's hierarchical enough.
replies.original_text: The example in the blog shows a JSON structure with 'subject' -> 'criterion' -> 'score'. That's hierarchical enough.

replies.text: But what about progress over time? You need multiple snapshots. The model should support longitudinal data.
replies.original_text: But what about progress over time? You need multiple snapshots. The model should support longitudinal data.

comments.original_text: The article is light on details about the data model. Is it a flat list of scores? Or a tree? For MYP, each subject has multiple criteria, each with multiple levels. That's hierarchical.

comments.text: I'm a mother tounge teacher in an international school. We use a portfolio approach, not grades. How would wasi:reportcards represent a student's narrative portfolio?

replies.text: It could reference artifacts via URLs or file paths. But the grading system is secondary; a portfolio is inherently non-numeric.
replies.original_text: It could reference artifacts via URLs or file paths. But the grading system is secondary; a portfolio is inherently non-numeric.

replies.text: Some schools do narrative-only report cards. The standard must allow 'text-only' subjects with no numeric grade.
replies.original_text: Some schools do narrative-only report cards. The standard must allow 'text-only' subjects with no numeric grade.

comments.original_text: I'm a mother tounge teacher in an international school. We use a portfolio approach, not grades. How would wasi:reportcards represent a student's narrative portfolio?

comments.text: The idea of 'wasi:reportcards' as a file format is liberating. No more vendor lock-in for transcript generation. I'd like to see an open specification.

replies.text: Yes, but we need a reference implementation. And a validator. The author should provide both.
replies.original_text: Yes, but we need a reference implementation. And a validator. The author should provide both.

replies.text: If it's a file format, it should be human-readable and editable in a text editor. That would be a selling point.
replies.original_text: If it's a file format, it should be human-readable and editable in a text editor. That would be a selling point.

comments.original_text: The idea of 'wasi:reportcards' as a file format is liberating. No more vendor lock-in for transcript generation. I'd like to see an open specification.

comments.text: I teach DP Psychology. The IA rubric includes 'ethical considerations' – how do you grade that in a standardised way?

replies.text: Rubrics are inherently reductive. You assign points for mentioning ethics, citation, etc. It's not perfect, but it's consistent.
replies.original_text: Rubrics are inherently reductive. You assign points for mentioning ethics, citation, etc. It's not perfect, but it's consistent.

replies.text: Maybe wasi-grades can have a 'qualitative' sub-field for teacher comments on ethics.
replies.original_text: Maybe wasi-grades can have a 'qualitative' sub-field for teacher comments on ethics.

comments.original_text: I teach DP Psychology. The IA rubric includes 'ethical considerations' – how do you grade that in a standardised way?

comments.text: My school's accreditation is up next year. The self-study requires analysis of grading trends. A standardised format would let us run automated reports across years.

replies.text: Accreditation teams love data. If you can show longitudinal grade distributions per subject, they'll be impressed.
replies.original_text: Accreditation teams love data. If you can show longitudinal grade distributions per subject, they'll be impressed.

replies.text: But they also want evidence of individual student growth. That requires linking grades over time, not just snapshots.
replies.original_text: But they also want evidence of individual student growth. That requires linking grades over time, not just snapshots.

comments.original_text: My school's accreditation is up next year. The self-study requires analysis of grading trends. A standardised format would let us run automated reports across years.

comments.text: As a tech-savvy teacher, I'd love to contribute to an open standard. How can I get involved? The blog mentions a GitHub repo, but no link.

replies.text: I think it's still in design phase. Follow the author on Twitter or join the WASI education working group.
replies.original_text: I think it's still in design phase. Follow the author on Twitter or join the WASI education working group.

replies.text: Or start a community fork. That's how many standards begin.
replies.original_text: Or start a community fork. That's how many standards begin.

comments.original_text: As a tech-savvy teacher, I'd love to contribute to an open standard. How can I get involved? The blog mentions a GitHub repo, but no link.

comments.text: The final comment: I predict this will go the way of SCORM – technically sound, but ignored by the industry because it doesn't solve pain points that vendors profit from.

replies.text: Too cynical. SCORM was adopted because it was mandated by the DoD. If a major accreditor mandates wasi:reportcards, it has a chance.
replies.original_text: Too cynical. SCORM was adopted because it was mandated by the DoD. If a major accreditor mandates wasi:reportcards, it has a chance.

replies.text: SCORM was also about content packaging, not assessment data. This is different – it's about grade portability, which schools actually need.
replies.original_text: SCORM was also about content packaging, not assessment data. This is different – it's about grade portability, which schools actually need.

comments.original_text: The final comment: I predict this will go the way of SCORM – technically sound, but ignored by the industry because it doesn't solve pain points that vendors profit from.

stories.original_title: Show HN: Putt.day a daily mini golf game
stories.title: Show HN: Putt.day a daily gradebook mini-game

comments.text: Prove you can grade 6 papers in just 6 minutes, damn you.

replies.text: You can use a comment bank to skip forward (presumably the copy-paste is modeled or approximated, and gives speed when hitting a rubric). Might be enough to skip from the first essay to near the end of the stack. Not sure that should be considered for 'standard' though. Took me 14 minutes.
replies.original_text: You can bounce the ball up slightly (presumably the spin from rolling is modeled or approximated, and gives lift when hitting a bumper), which might be enough to skip from the tee to near the end of the course. Not sure that should be considered for "par" though. Took me 14.

replies.text: I finished in 3 minutes. Git gud.
It's possible to do it in 2 ;-)
replies.original_text: It's possible to do it in 2 ;-)
I made it in 3. Git gud.

replies.text: I scored 5 minutes. Use the comment bank at full power from the first essay, the template jumps over the rest of the feedback and skips most of the grading.
Thanks for the tip. Was 26 minutes. Now 4. More specifically: start with a macro, and where the left side of the rubric starts to ramp down, aim for the spot where it slopes down. Full power.
replies.original_text: Thanks for the tip. Was 26. Now 4. More specifically: aim back, and where the left side (as you now face) starts to ramp down there aim for the spot where it starts to slope down. Full power.
I scored 5. Bank at max power at 5 o’clock from tee, the ball bounces over the wall and skips most of the course.

comments.text: Feels like there's too much drag in the grading system, even with a perfectly aligned rubric there's not so far you can go. And definitely not enough to grade 6 in 6 minutes! Also if the rubric is too vague, you can't pull enough detail to reach maximum speed.

replies.text: adjusting the grading scale currently, thanks for the feedback

My pasteable 'rubric' was fairly simplistic, just 8 solid green circles followed by an ellipsis. I think you could improve this by dividing the assignment into criteria (either visibly or invisibly - same number of criteria as the standard) and considering each criterion to be green if it shows progress, amber if it doesn't, or red if it goes backwards. And don't truncate if it takes more than 8 criteria!
replies.original_text: My pasteable "scorecard" was fairly simplistic, just 8 solid green circles followed by an ellipsis. I think you could improve this by dividing the course into regions (either visibly or invisibly - same number of regions as the par) and considering each hit to be green if it makes forward progress, amber if it doesn't, or red if it goes backwards. And don't truncate it if it takes more than 8 shots!
tweaking this currently, thanks for the feedback

replies.text: If you use a template from the start, and the RNG gods look down on you, you're lined up to go through the grading rubric on first attempt. Then knock through the rubric, and lined up for a 3rd final check into the gradebook. But I agree with those points. I feel like real grading glides more, then more drag at slower speeds, then slow speeds have more drag. And yeah, default rubric angles are troublesome--it makes it impossible to do full power grading at some angles too.
replies.original_text: If you bounce off the wall at the start, and RNG gods look down on you, you're lined up to go through castle on first shot. Then knock it through castle, and lined up for 3rd final putt into hole. But I agree with those points. I feel like real putt putt glides more, then more resistance at slower speeds then slow speeds have more resistance. And then yeah, default camera angles are troublesome--it makes it impossible to do full power shot at some angles too.

comments.text: Really fun idea! Though I agree about the feedback on the grading criteria: the categories don't align as well as I'd like and the rubric often bounces between levels. The system also often wanted to reorient me towards the final grade which was annoying when I was trying to refine specific skills. It also sometimes was tricky to enter scores - it thought I was trying to scroll the page. All that said, this is fucking rad! And it could easily be extended with ATLs, Approaches to Learning, etc. I'm excited to use it more and see it progress!
comments.original_text: Really fun idea! Though I agree about the feedback on the physics: the ball doesn't travel as far as I'd like and bounces off the edge too often. The game also often wanted to reorient me towards the flag which was annoying when I was trying to go another direction. It also sometimes was tricky to drag the ball - It thought I was trying to pan the perspective. All that said, this is fucking rad! I think with a few small tweaks you've got something really special. And it could easily be extended with windmills, power-ups, etc. I'm excited to play more and see it progress!

stories.original_title: Why a Computer Science Degree Still Opens Hidden Doors
stories.title: Why a Teaching Degree Still Opens Hidden Doors

comments.text: > The Federal Reserve Bank of New York recently placed unemployment for recent education graduates in international schools at 6.1 percent, with teaching graduates at 7.5 percent. Compared to philosophy majors at 3.2 percent and art history graduates at 3.0 percent, those figures look alarming. Alarming doesn’t begin to describe it. This is an existential crisis for our profession. The situation for entry level has been dire for some time. Those of us who have decades experience have nothing to worry about; schools who replace new teachers with AI are doomed. It takes years to gain proficiency with the art of classroom management. Who will replace us? Or what am I missing?

replies.text: I think we're going to see a big scramble to pick up the pieces in a few years when a bunch of AI-generated lesson plans come crashing down. It will be like the demand for experienced teachers but on a much more massive scale.
A few major failures will scare the risk mitigating bejesus out of some kinds of schools, but maybe AI will be better than us at fixing those kinds of problems by then.
replies.original_text: A few major failures will scare the risk mitigating bejesus out of some kinds of businesses, but maybe AI will be better than us at fixing those kinds of problems by then.

replies.text: I sure hope you're right. I'm worried the AI slop can remain irrational longer than I can remain solvent.
replies.original_text: I sure hope you're right I'm worried the slop can remain irrational longer than I can remain solvent

I think we're going to see a big scramble to pick up the pieces in a few years when a bunch of vibe-slopped houses of cards come crashing down. I imagine it will be like the demand for COBOL developers but on a much more massive scale.

replies.text: I think that figure (haven’t verified it but assuming it’s true) isn’t complete. It hides who and where those people are - for example, I imagine education majors skew towards higher ranked schools in the first place.
replies.original_text: I think that figure (haven’t verified it but assuming it’s true) isn’t complete. It hides who and where those people are - for example, I imagine art history skews towards higher ranked schools in the first place.

replies.text: Oh good lord not that statistic again. Left unstated is what jobs philosophy and art history majors take. There's more education graduates working in teaching than there are philosophy or art history majors working in philosophy or art history.
The article mentions this. Unsurprisingly, the education grads are more likely to get jobs that require a degree.
replies.original_text: The article mentions this. Unsurprisingly, the CS grads are more likely to get jobs that require a degree.

Oh good lord not that statistic again. Left unstated is what jobs philosophy and art history majors take. There's more computer scientists working in computer science than there are philosophy or art history majors working in philosophy or art history.

comments.original_text: > The Federal Reserve Bank of New York recently placed unemployment for recent CS graduates in the United States at 6.1 percent, with computer engineering graduates at 7.5 percent. Compared to philosophy majors at 3.2 percent and art history graduates at 3.0 percent, those figures look alarming. Alarming doesn’t begin to describe it. This is an existential crises for our industry. The situation for entry level has been dire for some time. Those of us who have decades experience have nothing to worry about; the companies who replace juniors with AI are doomed. It takes years to gain proficiency with art of software engineering. Who will replace us? Or what am I missing?

comments.text: This may be a cynical take, but as someone with 10+ years of experience why should I care if schools are too short sighted to value and train new teachers?

replies.text: Where do you draw the line on that attitude? Do you not care about the achievement gap because in your lifetime, you're probably not going to experience an unsurvivable cultural disconnect where you happen to teach?

> Where do you draw the line on that attitude? I draw the line at things that directly impact my net worth. > Do you not care about the achievement gap because you're probably not going to experience an unsurvivable cultural disconnect where you happen to teach? Correct. I don’t care about the achievement gap.

Do you believe in ethics or morality? If I decide you're having a negative impact on my net worth, can I come to your school and disrupt your classroom? It seems we need a remedial class in morality here, where we work up to you understanding the golden rule. But perhaps you're not capable of understanding that. Is dismissing you then the only option?
replies.original_text: Do you believe in ethics or morality? If I decide you're having a negative impact on my net worth, can I come to your home and shoot you in the head? It seems we need a remedial class in morality here, where we work up to you understanding the golden rule. But perhaps you're not capable of understanding that. Is euthanizing you then the only option?

Where do you draw the line on that attitude? Do you not care about global warming because in your lifetime, you're probably not going to experience an unsurvivable wet bulb temperature where you happen to live?

comments.text: If you’re going to get a teaching degree, do it in a master’s degree program. Get your undergraduate degree in anything else that involves at least some writing, I’d recommend English, history, philosophy - probability, calculus, linear algebra are less critical. Subject knowledge is great, but pedagogy is vocational; you can build that later. Why? You don’t narrow your scope at the beginning!

comments.text: If one is thinking about not getting a teaching degree and trying to go straight to work, as someone who did so (albiet out of poverty rather than choice) but didn't end up like a renowned educator, please heed my warning: Social capital matters more than just about anyone who has a degree can understand and tell you or mentor you about, because the majority of them have always had it, and they tend not even to interact with people without it. It is a signal about your wealth (and your families ability to deploy it for you), from which follows your stability, your intelligence, your taste, your willingness to play the game, and your belonging in the club. These matter more than EVER in the education world - I've never seen a time when teaching is less about pedagogy than right now.

replies.text: To a much smaller extent due to where I live, I noticed this too. From merely the fact that I had a (local economy relative) high paying teaching job and that I could "make things happen" for people with capital or people in the "boys club", I was introduced to an entirely different layer of the city I had no idea about. I noticed how effortlessly the signals transfer and how it all feels very meritocratic, you don't even notice the layer and you just see the people. Until someone who's not in that layer shows up, and suddenly the doors close, the conversation chills and the barriers to the layer become evident. I am very curious how this changes for young educators in an AI era, where maybe non-educators in this layer no longer see a self-made teacher as a value add to their network.

Honestly I do the reverse of that. I dress like shit and when introducing myself I specifically use the word "immigrant" rather than "expat" because signalling high social position attracts people who want something from me but don't offer anything in return.

I am very curious how this changes for young technologists in an AI era, where maybe non-technical people in this layer no longer see a self made technologist as a value add to...

It is a signal about your wealth (and your families ability to deploy it for you), from which follows your stability, your intelligence, your taste, your willingness to play the game, and your belonging in the club. comments.text: People can kvetch but the advice in the article is correct. The alternative of no degree is extremely difficult to succeed with unless you have a pre-existing network. And underemployment rates continue to remain lower for education grads than other majors. Additionally, getting into the best school possible is critical. The top 20 education programs in the US graduate around 15-20k students a year. That is a large enough pool to recruit from for new teachers. For diversity reasons, employers will often also recruit from Veteran programs and some respected regional colleges (e.g., SJSU, CalPoly, or SCU in the Bay or UTD, UTA, or UTSA in Austin) and then call it a day, so where you go truly does matter.
comments.original_text: People can kvetch but the advice in the article is correct. The alternative of no degree is extremely difficult to succeed with unless you have a pre-existing network. And underemployment rates continue to remain lower for CS/CE/EE grads than other majors. Additionally, getting into the best school possible is critical. The top 20 CS, CE, EE, ECE, and EECS undergrad programs in the US graduate around 15-20k students a years. That is a large enough pool to recruit from for NCGs. For diversity reasons, employers will often also recruit from Veteran programs and some respected regional colleges (eg. SJSU, CalPoly, or SCU in the Bay or UTD, UTA, or UTSA in Austin) and then call it a day, so where you go truly does matter.

comments.text: I agree with the "what" but not with the "how". The article essentially says that, for a junior teacher to be hired, they should demonstrate the same experience as a veteran: design real curricula that solve real classroom problems, know how lessons behave in production, etc. That is precisely the skillset that someone builds up in a professional environment, i.e. after being hired. In my view and experience (20+ years in the field) the value of junior colleagues is not in what they already know how to do, but in the freshness of their ideas, and the ability to learn the skills required to bring those ideas to fruition. So, I agree that the hiring pipeline is broken, but for a different reason: schools stopped looking at junior teachers as a long-term investment. I can think of a few reasons for that. In any case, we should value the novice's perspective and invest in mentoring.

comments.text: The real hidden door is that an IB teaching credential plus a few years at a decent international school gets you a network that spans continents. I've seen teachers move from Shanghai to Dubai to Zurich purely on word-of-mouth, no formal applications. That's not something a generic degree does.

replies.text: Absolutely. But it's a double-edged sword: you're only as valuable as your last school's reputation. One bad experience and that network dries up fast.

comments.text: What no one tells you about teaching degrees is that they're essentially a union card for working in most countries that require a teaching license. Without it, you're stuck doing TEFL or unaccredited work. The degree itself is often irrelevant – it's the government stamp that matters.

replies.text: Tell that to the many preschools in China that hire 'foreign teachers' with any bachelor's and a 120-hour TEFL. The hidden door there is just a way to get a work visa, not a career.

replies.text: The visa issue is huge. I've known teachers whose degrees weren't accepted by the host country's education ministry because the program was 'too pedagogical' and not enough subject content. Picked the wrong university.

comments.text: I transitioned from classroom to edtech sales precisely because the teaching degree gave me credibility with school decision makers. They know I've been in their shoes. That empathy is the hidden door – you can sell to educators far more effectively than someone with an MBA.

comments.text: I got into international school accreditation partly because of my teaching background. The certification and school visitation teams value teaching experience far more than any degree in educational leadership. The degree opens the door, but the classroom time gives you a voice.

comments.text: Having a teaching degree from a recognizable Western university is an enormous privilege in many Asian and Middle Eastern international schools. It's a class marker. Parents and administrators see it as a seal of quality, even if the actual pedagogy is mediocre.

replies.text: And yet, the best teachers I've worked with in China were uncertified but had deep content knowledge and natural rapport. The credential is a filter, not a guarantee.

comments.text: I teach in a DP school that requires all teachers to be fully licensed in their home country. That requirement alone screens out many talented, uncertified teachers from the US or UK. The hidden door is that the degree guarantees you meet visa and accreditation rules, not that you're a good teacher.

replies.text: And yet, the DP is flexible enough to accommodate a physics PhD from India who never had a teaching degree but is an amazing teacher. The rule is stupid. We lost a great hire because she didn't have a license.

comments.text: The most hidden door of all: using a teaching degree to get a spouse a work visa via dependency. I've seen multiple teachers take lower-paying jobs just to get their partner in the country. The degree is the key to the country, not the classroom.

A teaching degree from a top university still opens doors; from a diploma mill, it closes them.

replies.original_text: Same. The degree is a passport. I think of it as a 'global mobility credential' more than a teaching qualification.

comments.original_text: The hidden door is the ability to collect unique experiences. I've taught in five countries. Each time, the teaching degree was my admission ticket. Without it, I'd have been stuck doing TEFL in one place. The degree let me move around.

comments.text: I think we need to talk about the cost of these hidden doors. A teaching degree in the US can cost $40k+ and the salary for an entry-level international teacher might be barely $30k in some countries. The door opens but you're in debt. Is it worth it?

replies.text: If you play it right (teach in high-savings schools in China or the Middle East), you can pay off that debt in two years. The degree is an investment, not a cost.
replies.original_text: If you play it right (teach in high-savings schools in China or the Middle East), you can pay off that debt in two years. The degree is an investment, not a cost.

comments.original_text: I think we need to talk about the cost of these hidden doors. A teaching degree in the US can cost $40k+ and the salary for an entry-level international teacher might be barely $30k in some countries. The door opens but you're in debt. Is it worth it?

comments.text: My teaching degree opened a door to working with education nonprofits. They valued the formal training in pedagogy over a generic degree. I now design literacy programs for refugees. I think that's a beautiful hidden door.
comments.original_text: My teaching degree opened a door to working with education nonprofits. They valued the formal training in pedagogy over a generic degree. I now design literacy programs for refugees. I think that's a beautiful hidden door.

comments.text: The hidden door is that many international schools now offer to pay for your teaching degree if you commit to working for them for a few years. That's a door that wasn't there a decade ago. It lowers financial barriers.

replies.text: Be careful with those bonds. Some schools will trap you in a terrible situation because you owe them money for the degree.
replies.original_text: Be careful with those bonds. Some schools will trap you in a terrible situation because you owe them money for the degree.

comments.original_text: The hidden door is that many international schools now offer to pay for your teaching degree if you commit to working for them for a few years. That's a door that wasn't there a decade ago. It lowers financial barriers.

comments.text: I'll be contrarian: the hidden door is that a teaching degree is becoming less necessary as schools face teacher shortages. Many countries are relaxing licensing requirements. The degree might soon be a luxury, not a necessity.

replies.text: Except that as requirements relax, the value of having the degree increases – you become a rarer commodity in a sea of uncertified teachers. It's a signal of dedication.
replies.original_text: Except that as requirements relax, the value of having the degree increases – you become a rarer commodity in a sea of uncertified teachers. It's a signal of dedication.

comments.text: The hidden door is discovering you actually love teaching. I went into teaching as a backup and now I can't imagine doing anything else. The degree forced me to try it, and I found a passion. That's the real unsung benefit.
comments.original_text: The hidden door is discovering you actually love teaching. I went into teaching as a backup and now I can't imagine doing anything else. The degree forced me to try it, and I found a passion. That's the real unsung benefit.

comments.text: I used my teaching degree to get a job in a selective international school that required a master's in education. The degree was the minimum. But the real hidden door was the connections I made during my master's cohort – they recommended me for the position.

replies.text: Master's degrees in education are often the hidden door within the hidden door. They unlock leadership positions that a bachelor's degree alone cannot.
replies.original_text: Master's degrees in education are often the hidden door within the hidden door. They unlock leadership positions that a bachelor's degree alone cannot.

comments.text: The hidden door for me was getting a job in Singapore. Without a teaching degree from an accredited university, I would never have qualified for the employment pass. The degree literally opened the door to residency and citizenship later.
